Zhao, Shaojing
Huang, Li
Wang, Qing
Xiao, Jiafu
Lan, Minhuan
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) are novel zero-dimensional carbonaceous nanomaterial, and they are featured with an attractive combination of physical, chemical, and biomedical properties including wide absorption spectrum, tunable fluorescence, excellent water solubility, outstanding photostability, and biocompatibility, which leads to their diverse applications as optically functional materials in various fields, e.g., fluorescence sensing, bioimaging, and drug delivery. Recently, it was revealed that CDs could be used as an effective photosensitizer to generate reactive oxygen species under light excitation, and/or a photothermal agent to convert light energy into heat, which enable them to be applied in photodynamic therapy or photothermal therapy for cancer treatment. In this review, we summarize the latest progress of research on CDs in phototherapy, including their use as nanocarriers for organic phototherapy agents, and highlight the strategies which are adopted to improve the therapeutic efficacy of CDs-based phototheranostics. Furthermore, we also discuss the challenges and prospects of the CDs-based phototheranostics towards clinical applications. We hope this review will provide critical insights to inspire new exciting discoveries on CDs from both fundamental and practical views so as to prompt their advances in the phototherapy.

head.ce:title: Recent advances and prospects of carbon dots in phototherapy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this review, we summarize the latest progress of research on CDs in phototherapy, including their use as nanocarriers for organic phototherapy agents, and highlight the strategies which are adopted to improve the therapeutic efficacy of CDs-based phototheranostics. Furthermore, we also discuss the challenges and prospects of the CDs-based phototheranostics towards clinical applications.

Carbon dots (CDs) are novel zero-dimensional carbonaceous nanomaterial, and they are featured with an attractive combination of physical, chemical, and biomedical properties including wide absorption spectrum, tunable fluorescence, excellent water solubility, outstanding photostability, and biocompatibility, which leads to their diverse applications as optically functional materials in various fields, e.g., fluorescence sensing, bioimaging, and drug delivery. Recently, it was revealed that CDs could be used as an effective photosensitizer to generate reactive oxygen species under light excitation, and/or a photothermal agent to convert light energy into heat, which enable them to be applied in photodynamic therapy or photothermal therapy for cancer treatment. In this review, we summarize the latest progress of research on CDs in phototherapy, including their use as nanocarriers for organic phototherapy agents, and highlight the strategies which are adopted to improve the therapeutic efficacy of CDs-based phototheranostics. Furthermore, we also discuss the challenges and prospects of the CDs-based phototheranostics towards clinical applications. We hope this review will provide critical insights to inspire new exciting discoveries on CDs from both fundamental and practical views so as to prompt their advances in the phototherapy.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancers are a major public health issue, accounting for nearly 10 million deaths annually worldwide [1–3]. The current clinical treatments available for cancers, principally chemotherapy, radiotherapy, and surgery, commonly lead to sorts of side effects, including drug resistance, immune system imbalance, or postsurgical wound infections, which affects greatly the effectiveness of cure and rehabilitation of patients [4–6]. Therefore, great effort has been recently devoted to the development of new approaches with both a high therapeutic efficacy and low side effect. Phototherapy, including photodynamic therapy (PDT) and photothermal therapy (PTT), are emerging as promising treatment methods for cancers due to their non-invasive nature together with low toxicity, no drug resistance, and minimal side effects. Furthermore, phototherapy can be combined with other therapy methods, e.g., chemotherapy and immunotherapy, to minimize the potential side effects and enhance the opportunity of patients to be cured [7–12].

Fig. 1 shows the underlying mechanisms of PDT and PTT. When photosensitizers (PSs) or photothermal agents (PTAs) are exposed to light irradiation with proper wavelength, electrons are excited from the ground state (S0) to the excited singlet state (S1) due to the absorption of light energy, and then they would decay following three possible pathways: (1) to return from S1 to S0 through non-radiative relaxation, and energy is released in form of heat; (2) to return from S1 to S0 through radiative relaxation, producing fluorescence luminescence (FL); and (3) to transform from S1 to the excited triplet state (T1) through intersystem crossing. Subsequently, T1 could be inactivated through four routes: (i) to return from T1 to S0 through non-radiative relaxation with the release of heat; (ii) to return from T1 to S0 through radiative relaxation in accompany with the generation of phosphorescence luminescence (PL); (iii) to generate free radicals through electron transfer, and further to produce reactive oxygen species (ROS) such as hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), superoxide anion (O2 .-), and hydroxyl radicals (.OH) through reacting with oxygen (O2) or water (Type I electron transfer, PDT); or (iv) to sensitize O2 or H2O to produce singlet oxygen (1O2) through energy transfer (Type II energy transfer, PDT) [13]. Through excitation of the photosensitizers, different inactivation pathways of the excited states would lead to applications of the photosensitizers in different biomedical fields, e.g., FL and PL for bioimaging, heat for acoustic imaging and PTT, and generation of ROSs for PDT [14–17]. The increasing of intersystem crossing can significantly increase the yield of triplet excitons, which is helpful to sensitize O2 to generate 1O2 and enhance the PDT effect. However, ISC is a non-radiation process, and it has little effect on the photothermal conversion efficiency of CDs. Furthermore, their combination may also give rise to the development of new cancer treatment methods, e.g., PTT/PDT synergistic cancer therapy and photoacoustic/FL imaging-guided cancer therapy [18]. Because the ROS generated in PDT can efficiently inhibit the heat shock proteins, thereby promoting the effect of PTT, while the heat generated from PTT can dilate blood vessels, increasing the oxygen content in tumor cells and improving the effect of PDT.

Thus far, a variety of organic PSs, such as porphyrin [19], hypocrellin [20,21], and phthalocyanine [22] have been developed and used in PDT of cancers due to their high 1O2 generation quantum yield (QY) and controllable synthesis. Nevertheless, such organic PSs have the disadvantages of low water solubility, easy aggregation, and poor tumor-targeting capability [23–25]. Although gold nanostructures and semiconductor nanomaterials have strong absorbance in phototherapy window and high photothermal conversion efficiency, the high concentrations of heavy metal ions are highly biologically toxic, which limit their clinical application [26–28]. Therefore, the development of novel materials is essential for the phototherapy of cancer.

Carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials with sizes below 10 nm. They were firstly discovered by Xu et al. during the purification of carbon nanotube in 2004, and then named by Sun et al. due to their similar optical properties with silicon quantum dots (QDs)[29,30]. However, unlike the structure of traditional inorganic QDs, CDs are similar to organic molecules with large π-conjugated systems, i.e., the core of CDs is mainly graphitized sp2 carbon and the shell contains abundant organic functional groups, such as –COOH, –NH2, and –OH [31–33]. CDs have advantages of wide absorption spectrum, “tunable” emission wavelength, and excellent water solubility, biocompatibility, and photostability, making those “star molecules” in the field of optical functional materials with broad application prospects in FL sensing, bioimaging, and drug delivery [34–36]. More importantly, some CDs have the ability to convert light energy into ROS or heat and can therefore be applied as PSs or PTAs in PDT or PTT [37–40].

Herein, this review summarizes the latest progress of CDs in PDT, PTT, including CDs as nanocarriers or phototherapy agents in photodynamic, photothermal therapy and synergistic phototherapy. As shown in Fig. 2 , the top part represents example of CDs used in synergistic PDT and PTT, including the Ce6-RCDs and GNR@SiO2-CDs nanocomposites, Hypocrella bambusae-based CDs and Pyrene-derivated CDs. The lower left part represents the CDs act as carriers or PSs employed for PDT. The lower right part represents the CDs act as carriers or PTAs employed for PTT. Moreover, the advantages and drawbacks of different methods to synthesize CDs are summarized. The surface chemistry and element doping of CDs are analyzed. The strategies to enhance the characteristics especially optical properties of CDs are also discussed.

In addition, CDs can load PSs by covalently coupling or non-covalently adsorbing interaction. The strategies to improve the loading efficiency of PSs and enhance the efficiency of FL resonance energy transfer (FRET) are discussed. How to use the tumor microenvironment to achieve the controllable release of PSs is fully described. Novel technologies for the treatment of deep tumor are introduced, such as two-photon excitation (TPE) technology, biological resonance energy transfer (BRET), and chemical resonance energy transfer (CRET). Moreover, the key factors affecting the excitation and emission wavelengths, ROS generation and photothermal conversion efficiencies of CDs are clarified. Effective strategies for improving the hypoxic microenvironment of tumor and enhance the tumor targeting ability of CDs are proposed. The methods to prepare CDs with multi-modal imaging, i.e., fluorescence (FL), photoacoustic (PA), and magnetic resonance (MR) imaging, and photothermal/photodynamic synergistic therapy are systematically summarized. Furthermore, the challenges and prospects of the next-generation CDs-based phototheranostics for clinical applications are also discussed. We hope that this review will provide critical insights to inspire new exciting discoveries on CDs from both fundamental and practical views so that the realization of their potential in the phototherapy can be achieved.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis techniques for CDs employed for phototherapy
ce:section.ce:para: The properties of CDs are closely related to their preparation methods [41]. In general, there are two classified methods to prepare CDs, i.e., top-down and bottom-up. The top-down method is to decompose bulk carbon materials, such as graphite, coal, and candle ash into small CDs, while the bottom-up method is to carbonate and polymerize of organic molecules into CDs [42].

In top-down method, strong acid, such as sulphuric acid and nitric acid are usually used to oxidize the bulk carbon materials, and thus the surface of the obtained CDs contains a large number of carboxyl or hydroxyl groups [31–33]. The concentration of these oxygen-containing groups can be easily adjusted by using different acid. However, except to O, it is difficult to dope other elements into CDs. Furthermore, the strong acid may not only destroy the π-conjugated structure in CDs which lead to a short absorption and emission wavelengths, but also pollute the environment. The top-down method is not suitable for mass production of CDs.

While in the bottom-up method, CDs were prepared by solvothermal treatment of the organic molecules. In generally, this is a green method which needn’t toxic solvent or amount of strong acid or base. The reaction yield is much higher than the top-down methods, and thus is suitable for scalable synthesis. The surface chemical composition of the obtained CDs is mainly dependent on the chemical structure of carbon sources, the solvents, pH value, temperature, pressure, and reaction time. Kinds of elements, including N, S, P, B et al, even metal ions could be easily doped into CDs [36]. However, the doping concentration and doping sites are difficult to control.

The optical properties of CDs, especially their absorption and emission wavelengths are crucial for their phototherapy application. CDs with NIR fluorescence and strong NIR absorption could be used to effectively image and kill cancer cells in deep tissue [18]. Currently, there are three main luminescence mechanisms for CDs: (1) Core state emission [43]; (2) Surface state emission [44]; (3) Quantum size effect [45]. The core of CDs is mainly consisted by the sp2 hybridized carbon atoms, and the electronic transition is mainly π-π* which lead to a UV–visible absorption. By doping heteroatom with lone electron pairs, such as N, O, S, Se, some C = O, C = N, C-S, and C-Se can be introduced into the core of CDs, and n-π* transitions can also occur, resulting a red-shifting of the absorption and emission wavelengths [46]. Moreover, the optical properties of CDs can also be improved through the surface passivation by using PEG and PPEI-EI, etc. According to the “surface state emission” theory, with the surface modification of CDs by PEG or PPEI-EI etc., the surface defects of the CDs will be reduced, thereby increasing the fluorescence intensity [47–48].

The core of CDs is π-conjugated structure, while the surface of CDs has various hydrophilic groups, including –NH2, –OH, and –COOH. This special nanostructure of CDs endows them with broad application in drug delivery. Similar to chemotherapy drugs, molecular PSs, such as Ce6, porphyrin, phthalocyanine, etc. can be also efficiently loaded on the surface of CDs by covalent coupling or supramolecular interactions, including electrostatic interaction, π-π stacking, etc. [49,50]. The scheme of the interactions between CDs and photosensitizers are shown in Fig. 3 a. CDs have the following advantages as they act as nanocarriers for molecular PSs: (1) Good water solubility and biocompatibility, which can improve the stability and dispersibility of organic PSs in aqueous solution, thereby effectively avoiding FL quenching and a reduction in ROS production due to aggregation; (2) The wide absorption spectrum and tunable emission spectrum that can be exploited in a FRET system with other PSs, through which CDs can indirectly excite PSs and improve their ROS generation ability; (3) The large two-photon absorption cross section (TPACS) enables CDs have excellent two-photon excitation properties which can effectively kill cancer cells in deep tissues; (4) Through the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect of nanomaterials, PSs can be more efficiently targeted to tumor tissues.

In 2012, Chen et al. constructed a FRET system by covalently modifying Ce6 (a clinical used PS) on the surface of green fluorescent CDs, with a conjugated efficacy ~ 50% [51]. The PEG2000N was served as the surface passivation agent. The surface of the obtained CDs has number of amino groups, which can be covalently coupled with carboxyl group in the Ce6 by forming of amido bond. As shown in Fig. 3b, the FL spectrum of CDs and the absorption spectrum of Ce6 had a large overlap, meaning there is a highly effective FRET process from CDs to Ce6. Compared with the free Ce6, the CDs-Ce6 system shows stronger red FL, faster rate of ROS production, lower dark toxicity, and higher phototoxicity under 671-nm laser irradiation. The CDs-Ce6 system was efficiently enriched in the tumor site for FL imaging and PDT.

In addition to covalent coupling, molecular PSs can also be loaded onto CDs through supramolecular effects (such as electrostatic interactions, π–π stacking, etc.). For example, Zhu et al. designed a novel therapeutic drug through the electrostatic interaction between tetra-platinated porphyrin complex (PtPor) with four positive charges and carbon quantum dots (CQDs, a type of CDs) with negative charge (Fig. 3c)[52]. In the CQDs@PtPor system, CQDs transferred light energy to PtPor through the FRET process. Interestingly, CQDs@PtPor could produce more 1O2 than the pure PtPor under xenon lamp irradiation, indicating that CQDs@PtPor had a better PDT effect than PtPor. Kim et al. prepared CDs using α-cyclodextrin as carbon source in the presence of H2SO4 and HNO3 [53]. Subsequently, the surface of CDs was modified with NH2-PEG-NH2 and folic acid (FA) which can further improve the biocompatibility and tumor targeting ability of CDs. Furthermore, Zinc phthalocyanine (ZnPc) with large π-conjugated system was loaded onto the CDs whose cores have abundant sp2 hybrid carbon through π-π stacking interaction. The loading efficiency was calculated to be ~ 6 wt% (Fig. 3d). The obtained CD-PEG-FA/ZnPc NPs can effectively produce 1O2 in vitro under the 660-nm laser irradiation. The FL imaging in tumor bearing mice revealed that CD-PEG-FA/ZnPc NPs can effectively gather at the tumor tissue and exert the therapeutic effect of PDT.

CDs as nanocarriers can effectively improve the water solubility, biocompatibility, and the therapeutic effect of PSs. However, in these CDs-PS systems, light energy is indirectly transferred to PS through FRET process which may lead to energy loss. Tian et al. proposed several strategies to increase the utilization efficacy of light and improve efficiency of FRET, i.e., increase the fluorescence QY of CDs and shorten the distance between CDs and PSs. They prepared Mg, N-doped CDs with an ultra-high fluorescence QY of 84.6% using acrylic acid, 1,2-ethanediamine (EDA) and Mg(OH)2 as raw materials. Subsequently, Ce6 was covalently coupled with –NH2 group on the surface of CDs. In this CDs-Ce6 system, the high fluorescence QY of Mg, N-doped CDs enable most of the light energy can be transferred to Ce6. Meanwhile, the short distance between CDs and Ce6 can significantly enhance the FRET efficiency [54,55].

In addition, the light wavelength also has great effect on PDT [56–58]. However, most of the clinical used PSs have a weak absorption capacity in the phototherapy window (650–900 nm), leading to a limited capability in killing deep tumor tissue [59–61]. Two-photon excitation (TPE) technology uses near infrared (NIR) pulsed laser as the excitation light source. As compared with ordinary one-photon excitation (OPE), TPE has better tissue penetration ability. Additionally, TPE is a non-linear excitation mode, which can reduce damage to surrounding healthy tissues and achieve precise tumor therapy [62]. The larger the TPACS of the two-photon material, the better the two-photo absorption performance. The calculation formula of the TPACS of the sample is as follows:

R and S represent the reference and sample, respectively. F is the integral area of the TPE fluorescence spectrum; ψ is the OPE fluorescence quantum yield; η is the overall fluorescence collection efficiency of the experimental apparatus; and C is the concentration. The common unit of the TPACS is GM, 1 GM = 10-5 cm4 s photon-1 molecule-1 [63].

However, the TPACS of clinical used PSs (less than100 GM) is too small to efficiently exert the superiority of TPE technology [64]. In contrast, CDs, which possess a large π-conjugated system, have much higher TPACS (>105 GM) than that of molecular PSs [63]. Chen et al. loaded the positively charged PS, 5,10,15,20-Tetrakis(1-methyl-4-pyridinio)porphyrin tetra(p-toluenesulfonate) (TMPyP) on the surface of negatively charged CDots through electrostatic interaction. As revealed in Fig. 3e, TMPyP can be excited indirectly through FRET process as the absorption spectrum of TMPyP and the emission spectrum of CDots have a large overlap. Additionally, CDots have a large TPACS (up to 15,000 GM at 700 nm) allowing CDots-TMPyP to efficiently generate 1O2 and kill Hela cells under the TPE of a 700 nm laser [65].

Although the TPE technology can kill deep cancer cells, it requires expensive pulse lasers. Recently, chemiluminescence/bioluminescence functional materials were designed to combine with molecular PSs and constructed chemiluminescence resonance energy transfer (CRET) or bioluminescence resonance energy transfer (BRET) system to achieve a high treatment effect for deep tumors without the external light source [66]. Firefly luciferase can catalyze substrate luciferin in the presence of Mg2+, ATP, and O2 to produce FL; therefore, this system could be exploited as an internal light source for PDT [67]. For example, Li et al. prepared a CDs-PIX complex by covalently modifying PIX (an organic PS) on the surface of CDs (Fig. 4 a). SMMC-7721 cancer cells were cultured in the solution containing CDs-PIX, Mg2+, ATP, firefly luciferase, and luciferin. The bioluminescence from the luciferin can excite the CDs through a BRET process, while the excited CDs further sensitize PIX to generate 1O2 through another FRET process [68]. However, it needs two energy transfer steps to excite the PS, which significantly decrease the therapeutic efficacy. To address this issue, Tian et al. prepared yellow emission CDs (y-CDs) using 1,2,4-triaminobenzene dihydrochloride as a precursor through a hydrothermal method. Ce6 was loaded onto y-CDs by covalent coupling to form y-CDs-Ce6. As shown in Fig. 4b, Luminol was oxidized by H2O2 under the catalysis of horseradish peroxidase (HRP) to produce blue chemiluminescence which can simultaneously excite Ce6 and CDs, and the excited CDs can further sensitize Ce6. Compared with the BRET method above, there are two pathways to excite the PS, which can effectively increase the energy utilization efficiency and improve the therapy efficacy [69].

In this part, we summarized the different approaches for CDs loading PSs by covalent coupling, electrostatic interaction, and π-π stacking, and compared their loading efficiency. Meanwhile, we also discussed several methods to activate PSs, including TPE, BRET and CRET, which can effectively kill cancer cells in deep tissue.

CDs can be used not only as nanocarriers for molecular PSs in PDT but also to produce ROS themselves under light irradiation. Markovic et al. prepared graphene quantum dots (GQDs, a type of CDs) which can produce 1O2 under blue light (470 nm) irradiation for the PDT of cancer cells [70]. However, blue light have poor penetration ability in tissue [71–73]. Zhao et al. prepared red emission CDs (R-CDs) using phosphoric acid and o-phenylenediamine as precursors through a hydrothermal method. The R-CDs were able to generate 1O2 under the irradiation of a 532-nm laser, which can be applied for the PDT of A549 cancer cells and tumors in mice [40]. Bi et al. used acrylic acid and Cu(NO3)2 as raw materials to obtain Cu-complexed polyacrylic acid through in-situ polymerization and then Cu-doped CDs (Cu-CDs) were obtained through the pyrolysis of Cu-complexed polyacrylic acid. The Cu-CDs could produce 1O2 under the irradiation of a LED lamp (400–700 nm) with a QY of 36% [74].

However, the UV–vis light has poor tissue penetration ability, especially the UV light may cause certain damage to human body, while the NIR light has excellent tissue penetration ability, which is helpful for killing cancer cells in the deep tissue. Recently, Wang et al. prepared positively charged CDs using polythiophene as a precursor through a hydrothermal method. Subsequently, using the positively charged CDs as a component and negatively charged amphiphilic sodium dodecylbenzene sulfonate (SDBS) as a cross-linking agent, they produced self-assembled CD nanospheres (CDNS) through electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions between CDs and SDBS. After modifying with HOOC-PEG-NH2, the obtained CDNS could be intravenously injected (Fig. 5 a). Compared with pure CDs, the absorption of CDNS was red-shifted by ~ 50 nm with a wide absorption range (400–800 nm). More importantly, CDNS were able to produce 1O2 under the 671-nm laser irradiation with a 1O2 QY of 45%, and they had a more obvious 4 T1 cancer cells killing effect than CDs. In vivo FL imaging of tumor bearing mice indicated that CDNS can be well accumulated at the tumor site and led to a reduction in tumor volume, demonstrating that CDNS also had an excellent PDT effect in vivo [75].

In addition to the excitation wavelength, the 1O2 QY is another important parameter in evaluating the performance of PSs. The higher the QY of 1O2, the greater the efficiency in killing cancer cells [76–78]. The common methods to detect ROS include UV–visible absorption, fluorescence, and Electron Spin Resonance (ESR) spectroscopies. Due to the high activity and short lifetime of ROS, it is difficult to directly detect the ROS, and thus kinds of chemical trapping agents are developed to capture the ROS, as summarized in Table 1 . For example, in the ESR method, TEMP and BMPO are used as the trapping agents for 1O2, and ·OH, respectively. The generated ROS by CDs can sensitively and selectively react with these trapping agents and induce the variation of the ESR spectra [79]. In UV–visible absorption method, ADPA and DPBF can be oxide by 1O2, NBT is oxide by O2 ·-, leading to the decreasing of their absorbance [52]. While in fluorescence method, SOSG and terephthalic acid can react with 1O2 and ·OH, respectively, and then produce strong green and blue fluorescent compounds [51]. In addition, 1O2 has a characteristic phosphorescence peaked at ~ 1270 nm [79].

The 1O2 generation quantum yield of CDs could be calculated by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence method according the following formula:[79]

where KCDs and KR are the decomposition rate constants of trapping agents in the presence of CDs and reference. ACDs and AR represent the absorbance of the CDs and reference, respectively. ΦCDs and ΦR are the 1O2 generation quantum yield of CDs and reference, respectively. In addition, the characteristic phosphorescence of 1O2 could also be used to measure its quantum yield according the following formula:[79]
(3)

where ICDs and IR represent the phosphorescence peak area of 1O2 produced by the CDs and reference, respectively. The common reference photosensitizers are RB(Φ = 75%), MB(Φ = 52%), ICG(Φ = 0.8%) and IR783(Φ = 0.7%) [80–83]. And the 1O2 QY of CDs served as photosensitizers are summarized in Table 2 according to the available examples in this review. Although some CDs is with lower 1O2 QY than that of some reference photosensitizers, they have obvious advantages in other aspects such as water solubility, biocompatibility, NIR absorption, targeting, etc. Therefore, the CDs photosensitizers can still have an excellent application in biomedical field.

For example, some organic PSs (such as DPP, 5,10,15,20-Tetraphenylporphyrin (TPP), phthalocyanine, etc.) have excellent photostability and a high 1O2 QY, but their water solubility is poor, which makes them easily aggregate in water and reduces their ability to produce 1O2 [80–82]. While chitosan has good water solubility and biocompatibility and can be used along with these organic PSs to improve their performance in phototherapeutic applications. Xie et al. synthesized blue-emitting DPP-CDs using DPP and chitosan as precursors (Fig. 5b). Compared to the pure DPP, the DPP-CDs not only retained the ability to produce 1O2 (QY = 27.6%), but also exhibited improved water solubility and biocompatibility. Cell viability and tumor bearing mice model experiments demonstrated that the DPP-CDs can efficiently kill HepG2 cancer cells and inhibit tumor growth under 540-nm laser irradiation [80]. Similarly, they further synthesized porphyrin-based CDs (TPP-CDs) using TPP and chitosan as precursors through a solvothermal method (Fig. 5c). The TPP-CDs exhibited strong blue FL and good water solubility as there were abundant hydroxyl groups on the surface of TPP-CDs [81]. Additionally, TPP-CDs show better 1O2 generation ability than the pure TPP. Recently, Wang et al. synthesized red-emitting CDs using pheophytin as the carbon source and dimethyl formamidine (DMF) as the solvent by using a microwave-assisted hydrothermal method. The obtained hydrophobic CDs had a maximum NIR emission peak at ~ 680 nm, and high 1O2 QY of 62% under 671-nm laser irradiation. Furthermore, these CDs were assembled with DSPE-PEG2000 to increase their water solubility. The obtained CDs-PEG assembly retained efficient 1O2 generation and strong NIR FL properties (Fig. 5d)[83].

Through the process of inactivating the excited state of the PSs, the production of 1O2 (ET(1) in Fig. 6 a, left) and FL are competed and show a trade-off relationship, leading to a theoretical maximum 1O2 QY of 100%. Wang et al. found that a PS with an energy gap ΔEST (energy gap between S1 and T1) higher than the energy (~0.98 eV) required to sensitize O2 to produce 1O2 could be obtained by regulating the energy levels between S1 andT1. Therefore, the intersystem crossing process (ET(2) in Fig. 6a, right) from S1 to T1 can also produce sufficient energy to sensitize O2 to generate 1O2. Based on this, Wang’s group proposed a “multiple sensitization mechanism” to generate 1O2. In detail, there are two pathways for sensitizing O2 to generate 1O2: one is the conventional pathway through the energy transfer from T1 to S0, the other pathway is through the energy transfer from S1 to T1, and thus the theoretical maximum of 1O2 QY can reach up to 200%, which break through the theoretical maximum value of 1O2 QY (QYmax = 100%) of the conventional PSs. Based on this multiple sensitization mechanism, the group synthesized N,S-codoped GQDs using a polythiophene derivative as the precursor by a hydrothermal method. The obtained GQDs exhibited good water solubility, biocompatibility, and photostability. In addition, the GQDs had a wide absorption range (400–700 nm) and a red emission at 680 nm. More importantly, the 1O2 QY of the GQDs reached up to 130%, the highest value reported in the literature [79]. The in vitro and in vivo studies demonstrated that the GQDs can be used as FL imaging guided PDT agents and provide a highly efficient cancer therapy.

In order to kill cancer cells accurately and efficiently, there is a need to prepare CDs with the ability to target cancer cells and undergo PDT. Shen et al. prepared CDs using hyaluronic acid (HA) as a precursor by a hydrothermal method. The surface of the CDs retained the structure of HA, thus being able to actively target cancer cells overexpressing the CD44 receptor. More importantly, the CDs were able to generate superoxide free radicals, thus effectively inhibiting the growth of 4 T1 cancer cells (Fig. 6b) [84]. The hypoxic microenvironment of tumor tissue is not conducive to the O2-dependent process of generating 1O2 [85], while the tumor blood vessels would be destroyed after PDT, which further reduces the O2 concentration in the tumor area [86]. Moreover, the extremely hypoxic environment may induce the recovery, invasion, and metastasis of cancer cells [87]. To improve the O2 concentration in tumor, Wang et al. synthesized hydrophobic Mn-CDs using manganese (II) phthalocyanine as a precursor (Fig. 6c). The Mn-CDs had a maximum FL emission peak at 745 nm and the presence of Mn endowed them with the capacity to be used for magnetic resonance (MR) imaging. Following the self-assembly of Mn-CDs and DSPE-PEG2000, the Mn-CDs had good water solubility and could be applied for FL and MR dual-modal imaging. Moreover, Mn-CDs retained the capacity of phthalocyanine to generate 1O2 with a high QY of 40% under 635-nm laser irradiation. Importantly, Mn-CDs could also efficiently catalyze H2O2 to generate O2 in an acidic tumor microenvironment, thus improving the issue of hypoxia in solid tumor and promoting the PDT effect [82].

In this section, the general methods for detecting ROS and calculating 1O2 generation quantum yield are summarized. Furthermore, several important factors for evaluating the performance of PSs, the approaches to optimize the therapeutic efficacy of PSs are discussed.

PTT is an O2-independent process that can convert light energy into heat by PTAs, thereby killing cancer cells. PTT has unique advantages in the treatment of hypoxic tumors. CDs can also play the role of nanocarriers and PTAs in PTT. When CDs are served as nanocarriers for PTAs, the controlled release of PTAs can be realized by taking advantage of the special tumor microenvironment. In addition, the special optical properties of CDs also help to achieve fluorescence imaging-guided photothermal therapy. As shown in Fig. 7 , Cheong et al. synthesized the pH/redox responsive CDs and loaded IR825 through π-π stacking and hydrophobic interactions. In CDs-IR825 complex, the fluorescence of IR-825 was quenched by CDs. However, when the complex enters the tumor site, the imine bond and disulfide bond were destroyed in the presence of acid and GSH, respectively, fully releasing IR825 and switching on the FL. At this time, the PTT effect of IR825 can also be achieved under 808-nm laser irradiation [88].

CDs have a broad absorption spectrum and a strong capacity to absorb light and are therefore excellent PTAs for PTT. The photothermal conversion efficiency (η) is an important parameter to evaluate the performance of photothermal agents. The formula for calculating the η is as follows [63]:
(4)
η = hS

where h is the heat-transfer coefficient, S is the surface area of the container, Tmax is the equilibrium temperature, TSurr is room temperature, QDis is the heat dissipation due to the light absorbed by the quartz sample cell, I is the laser power, and A is the absorbance at the wavelength of the used laser. The value of hS was derived according to the following equation:
(5)

Here, τs is the sample system time constant, and mD and CD are the mass and heat capacity of deionized water, respectively.

Lin et al. prepared R-CDs with a FL QY of 22.9% using citric acid as a precursor and formamide as the solvent by a microwave-assisted solvothermal method. R-CDs tended to accumulate in the nucleus and thus were able to deliver chemotherapeutic drugs to the nucleus. In addition, R-CDs exhibited a high photothermal conversion efficiency (η = 43.9%) under 671-nm laser irradiation, and thus were used as multifunctional diagnostic agents for cancer treatment (Fig. 8 a)[89]. Recently, Li et al. developed biocompatible N-O-CDs using 1,3,6-trinitropyrene (TNP) and an N-containing polymer of branched polyethylenimine (BPEI) as precursors. The N-O-CDs exhibited a high photothermal conversion efficiency (η = 38.3%) under a low power density NIR laser (808 nm, 0.8 W/cm2)[90]. The doping of metal ions into CDs is an important strategy to improve the NIR absorption and photothermal conversion efficiency of CDs. Wu et al. fabricated Cu/CD-crosslinked nanosheets (CuCD NSs) by mixing Cu2+ and S-doped CDs through a hydrothermal method. The CuCD NSs exhibited excellent NIR absorption and a photothermal conversion efficiency of 41.3%, enable them have a good therapeutic effect both in vivo and in vitro. The CDs also functioned in three-modal imaging (Photothermal (PT), Photoacoustic (PA) and FL imaging), which could be applied for the visual diagnosis and treatment of cancer (Fig. 8b)[91].

In addition to small organic molecules, polymers can also be used to prepare CDs with PTT properties. Wang’s group prepared red emissive CDs using polythiophene phenylpropionic acid (PPA) as the precursor. The CDs had a wide absorption spectrum, good photostability, and a high photothermal conversion efficiency (η = 38.5%) under 671-nm laser irradiation, which is suitable for FL and PA dual-model imaging-guided PTT [92]. In order to further improve the photothermal conversion efficiency of CDs, Wang et al. introduced the Se into the CDs to prepare S,Se-co-doped CDs with high photothermal efficiency (58.2%) and no 1O2 generation (Fig. 8c). They proposed that the large π-π conjugated structure of CDs resulting a low energy gap (ES1–T1) of less than 0.84 eV. While the S1 energy of the S, Se-codoped CDs was calculated to be ~ 1.51 eV, and the T1 energy was accordingly estimated to be less than 0.67 eV, which is lower than the energy required for sensitizing ground-state O2 (0.95 eV). Therefore, the S, Se-codoped CDs unable generated 1O2, and the absorbed light energy was mainly converted into heat to obtained a high photothermal conversion efficiency.

Because of the good biocompatibility of biomass, the CDs produced by biomass usually have similar properties, so it is very suitable for the biomedical field. Hao et al. prepared CDs with NIR-II FL using watermelon juice as a carbon source by a hydrothermal method. The CDs had an ultra-small particle size, good biocompatibility, and a wide absorption spectrum. Furthermore, the CDs were easily excreted from the kidneys, i.e., 65% of the CDs were excreted through the urine within 6 h. In addition, these CDs had a high photothermal conversion efficiency (η = 30.6%) and a strong FL in the range 900–1200 nm under 808-nm laser irradiation, which can be used for NIR-II FL imaging-guided PTT (Fig. 8d). At a CD concentration of 20 mg/mL, the viability of HeLa cells was higher than 80% in dark condition, while it was decrease to less than 10% under 808-nm laser irradiation. This indicated that the CDs have a low dark toxicity and high phototoxicity. The tumor volume in mice had shrunk obviously within 6 days, indicating that the CDs also had a good cancer cell killing effect in vivo [93].

Herein, we introduced the method to calculate photothermal conversion efficiency, summarized and classified the CDs employed as photothermal agents by their carbon sources, i.e., small organic molecules, polymers and biomass. Furthermore, the effect of doping on the photothermal conversion efficiency of CDs is also discussed.

The hypoxic microenvironment of a tumor will affect the efficiency of PDT, while the heat-shock protein will also influence the therapeutic effect of PTT [94–96]. While in the combination of PDT and PTT, the heat generated by CDs may increase the O2 concentration, and the ROS generated by CDs can destroy heat-shock protein, thereby achieving a “1 + 1 > 2″ cooperative treatment effect [97]. Therefore, developing multifunctional materials with both a PDT and a PTT effect can greatly improve the therapeutic efficacy of malignant tumors.

CDs can be used as both the nanocarriers of PSs or PTAs and as the phototherapy agents themselves. Wang et al. synthesized multifunctional gold nanorod@silica-CDs (GNR@SiO2-CDs) by loading positively charged CDs which were prepared from a cationic polythiophene (PT2, Fig. 8c) on the negatively charged GNRs through electrostatic interaction. The GNRs were used for both PTT and PA imaging (Fig. 9 a), and the CDs were applied for PDT and FL imaging. The wrapped SiO2 layer led to an improvement of the chemical stability of GNRs and CDs whilst preventing the FL of CDs from being completely quenched by the GNRs. By combining these three components, the prepared GNR@SiO2-CDs can perform PDT (under 635-nm laser irradiation) and PTT (under 808-nm laser irradiation) stably and efficiently. The size of GNR@SiO2-CDs was about 90 nm which enables them to accumulate in the tumor sites through the enhanced permeability and retention (ERP) effect after intravenous injection. Compared with single CDs or GNRs, GNR@SiO2-CDs lead to a more effective therapeutic effect under a lower power light [98]. However, it is not convenient to use two light sources simultaneously. In contrast, using a single light source to achieve PDT and PTT simultaneously has obvious advantage. Lin et al. prepared Ce6-RCDs by covalently coupling red emissive CDs (RCDs) with Ce6 at a loading efficiency of 0.56% (Fig. 9b). RCDs acted as PTAs and the photothermal conversion efficiency reached 46%, while Ce6 played the role of PS, generating 1O2 under 671-nm laser irradiation. Therefore, compared to the equivalent RCDs or Ce6 alone, the Ce6-RCDs system exhibited a much higher cancer therapy efficacy under a low laser power density (671 nm, 0.50 W/cm2). Furthermore, the Ce6-RCDs also exhibited multi-modal imaging capabilities (FL/PA/PT imaging), which can be used for guidance of the phototherapeutic process [99].

However, in the above methods, the CDs need to be combined with PTAs or PSs to form composite nanomaterials. The complicated environment in the organism may affect the stability of these composite nanomaterials, and the physical and chemical properties of these additional PTAs or PSs may also influence the CDs. Therefore, it is necessary to develop CDs that can simultaneously perform PDT and PTT. Using polythiophene benzoic acid as a precursor, Wang’s group prepared CDs which can simultaneously generated 1O2 (QY = 27%) and heat (η = 36.2%) under 635-nm laser irradiation [100]. Recently, Wang et al. synthesized CDs using Hypocrella bambusae as the precursor. The obtained Hypocrella bambusae CDs (HBCDs) had a good water solubility, low toxicity, broad absorption spectrum (350–800 nm), and strong red FL. More importantly, the CDs had a high 1O2 QY (QY = 38%) and good photothermal conversion efficiency (η = 27.6%). These characteristics of HBCDs made them possible to achieve FL/PA imaging-guided synergistic PDT and PTT (Fig. 10 a)[101].

In order to obtain CDs with higher light utilization, Wang’s group used pyrene derivatives with strong electron-withdrawing groups as the carbon source to prepare CDs which able to generate OH·, 1O2 (QY = 5.7%), and heat simultaneously (Fig. 10b). Of note, the photothermal conversion efficiency of CDs is as high as 73.5%. The yellow fluorescent CDs had a lysosome targeting function and can be used for FL/PA imaging-guided synergistic PDT and PTT [102]. Furthermore, Na2SO3, which has oxidizing capability, was introduced in the process of preparing CDs. The FL emission peak of the obtained CDs was blue-shifted from 570 nm to 490 nm and the FL QY also increased from 4.9% to 49%. Additionally, the CDs had a large TAPCS and were able to generate 1O2 (QY = 7.2%) and heat (η = 52.3%) under NIR laser irradiation (Fig. 10c). It was proposed that, during the process of carbonization, the addition of oxidizing Na2SO3 leads to an increase in the number of O2-containing functional groups on the surface of CDs which can increase their FL QY. Furthermore, the large π-conjugated structure is destroyed by the oxidation of Na2SO3, resulting in a blue shift of the FL spectrum [103].

To improve the NIR absorption of CDs, Shen et al. synthesized Cu,N-co-doped CDs using EDTA·2Na and CuCl2 as raw materials through a hydrothermal method (Fig. 10d). Cu(II) was present in the Cu, N co-doped CDs through the complexation of N-Cu–N, leading to a strong absorption of CDs at 737 nm. More importantly, the Cu,N-co-doped CDs were able to generate 1O2 and heat under 808-nm laser irradiation [104].

In this part, we summarized the CDs used in synergistic phototherapy. i.e., CDs can be used as either nanocarriers to load PSs and PTAs, or as phototherapy agents. Significantly, some of CDs show both ROS and heat generating capability, and achieve the synergistic therapy of PDT and PTT.

CDs have been attracted considerable attention in bioimaging, chemosensing, drug delivery, photocatalysis, and phototherapy [105–113]. In this review, we summarize the latest progress of CDs in PDT, PTT, including CDs as nanocarriers or phototherapy agents in photodynamic, photothermal therapy and synergistic phototherapy. Firstly, the synthesis methods and modification strategies (surface chemistry, element doping, etc.) of CDs for phototherapy are analyzed. CDs can be served as the nanocarriers for photosensitizers, the interaction between CDs and photosensitizers are discussed in detail. Meanwhile, several different light sources (TPE, BRET, CRET) used to enhance the treatment effect of deep tumors are summarized. While CDs served as phototherapy agents, the strategies to improve the NIR absorption, 1O2 quantum yield, photothermal conversion efficiency, and targeting ability of CDs are summarized. Moreover, the method to improve the hypoxia in tumor cells are proposed. Furthermore, the synthesis strategies for CDs with the characteristics of imaging (FL/PA/MR imaging) and synergistic phototherapy are also summarized. Although great progresses have been achieved, the practical applications remain difficult and challenging.
Theoretical analysis of the relationship between nanostructures and properties of CDs. Currently, the research on the relationship between nanostructures and properties of CDs is still at the basic stage and there are many problems and technical challenges. For example, transmission electron microscope, atomic force microscope, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, Raman spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction, and other conventional characterization methods cannot accurately analyze the nanostructure of CDs, especially the doping sites, doping patterns, and doping concentration of heteroatoms in CDs cannot be exactly determined. Moreover, the luminescence mechanism of CDs is still unclear. The relationship between the nanostructure and photochemical/photophysical properties of CDs needs to be further clarified. The properties of CDs and the characteristics of their precursors are closely related. Designing and synthesizing novel organic molecule and using it as a single carbon source to prepare CDs can help us to better understand the relationship between the structure and properties of CDs. Although the HOMO/LUMO, bandgap, and surface state theories have been developed to explain the electronic characteristics and luminescence mechanism of CDs, more mechanisms still need to be developed to guide the adjustment of the NIR absorption/emission of CDs and improve the fluorescence QY of CDs.
Large-scale preparation and controllable surface modification of CDs. At present, the reported synthesis method of CDs is mainly solvothermal method which often faces the problem of heterogeneity in the process of large-scale preparation of CDs. Meanwhile, the surface chemical composition of CDs may change during the purification of CDs, thereby affecting its properties. In addition, although the surface functional groups of CDs are abundant so that it can be easy to modify the CDs. It also leads to poor reproducibility of the surface modification of CDs and it is difficult to precisely control the reaction sites of surface modification and the concentration of modifiers. Therefore, there remain many challenges about how to accurately control the micro-nano structure, morphology, size distribution, doping behavior, and the surface chemical composition of CDs.
Purification and quantification of CDs. The CDs prepared from top-down or bottom-up are usually a mixture with different size distribution and chemical compositions. The commonly used purification methods are mainly included centrifugation, filtration, dialysis, and column chromatography. Centrifugation and filtration are used to remove the large aggregated particles and insoluble carbides. Dialysis can remove the small molecules or polymers, and obtain CDs with different size distributions by using different dialysis bags. Column chromatography can separate CDs with different surface chemistry compositions. These methods are usually used in combination. However, even after strict purification procedures, the resulting CDs are still mixtures. Most reported CDs are quantified by mass concentration.
When CDs are used as the carriers of PSs, there are two main challenging. One is about how to improve the loading efficiency of PSs and achieve its controlled release in tumor tissues. The other is about how to improve the stability of the CDs-PSs composite system during circulation in vivo and its tumor targeting ability to avoid phototherapy agents being leaked before reaching the tumor tissue. Modification of bionic materials and targeting probe on the surface of CDs are expected to increase the uptake rate of CDs by tumor cells.
When CDs are used as PSs or PTAs themselves, it is also an important issue to precisely regulate its excitation state and inactivation pathways. In the process of inactivation of the excited state of CDs, FL, PL, ROS generation, and photothermal conversion are competing. And it is a great challenging issue in the scientific fields of chemistry, materials, life, etc. to develop CDs phototheranostics agents with the characteristics of the above all, and can meet the clinical requirements of diagnosis and treatment.
Developing CDs with upconversion fluorescence properties [114]. Most of clinically used organic PSs have a weak absorption in NIR region, but a strong absorption in UV–visible region. CDs with upconversion fluorescence characteristics, which can absorb long-wavelength light and then emit short-wavelength light which could excite the carried organic PSs by FRET mechanism and then kill cancer cells in deep tissue. Although some CDs showed upconversion fluorescence, the upconversion efficiency is low, resulting a low FRET efficiency and poor therapeutic efficacy.
Overcoming the tumor hypoxia. The hypoxic microenvironment of tumor hinders the O2-dependent PDT process, and the blood vessels of the tumor are destroyed after PDT, further reducing the O2 concentration in the tumor area. In addition, the extremely hypoxic environment may induce the recovery, invasion, and metastasis of cancer cells. Using the tumor microenvironment, such as high concentration of GSH, H2O2 and low pH to generate O2 in situ is the key to solving tumor hypoxia and improving the efficiency of PDT. Some reported CDs have the catalytic activity of natural enzymes including peroxidase, oxidase and catalase [115]. However, how to regulate the catalytic activity of CDs to achieve efficient treatment of tumors still faces huge challenges.
Improving the therapeutic effect on the deep tumor. Most of the currently reported CDs have a main absorption wavelength in the visible light region, weak absorption in the phototherapy window and basically no absorption in the NIR-II region, which resulting in their inability to effectively treat deep tumor. Although multi-photon excitation technology has good penetration ability, high-power strong pulse lasers have many inconveniences in clinical use. Meanwhile, the energy transfer efficiency of bioluminescence and chemiluminescence is not high which resulting in limited therapeutic effects. In order to maximize the effect of treating deep tumors as much as possible, combining PDT, PTT, sonodynamic therapy, electrodynamic therapy, chemotherapy, gene therapy, immunotherapy and other treatment methods is expected to achieve the effect of “a whole great than the sum of the parts”[116–122].
Synergistic phototherapy and immunotherapy based on CDs. Particularly, immunotherapy is a new therapeutic modality for cancer treatment by harnessing the power of patients’ own immune system. Compared with single phototherapy or immunotherapy, immunotherapy and phototherapy have a certain promoting effect on each other [123,124]. There is only one report concerning CDs application for immunotherapy. i.e., Wu et al. used CDs as immune adjuvants to efficiently enhance cancer immunotherapy [125]. It will be meaningful to design CDs-based nanoplatform to realize the synergistic cancer treatment of phototherapy and immunotherapy.
Evaluating the biosafety of CDs. Carbon nanomaterials have very stable structure and are difficult to effectively degrade in human body. The long-term biosafety in clinical use and metabolic behavior in the body of CDs require further systematic evaluation. Researches have shown that the biological toxicity of carbon nanomaterials is related to its morphology, size, and surface chemical composition. However, it is still not clear how to degrade or excrete carbon nanomaterials from the organism and eliminate their toxicity [126,127].

Although CDs still have many challenges in the clinical application of phototherapy, with the rapid development of biology, chemistry, nanomedicine and related fields, the above problems will be gradually solved and it is expected to achieve early diagnosis and personalized treatment of cancer.
